narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 25, Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 52% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This close-to-even odds tipped the decision toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants with stronger predicted chances of bar passage to maintain high success rates. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class (average 5.53), signaling to the model a performance trajectory that correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance, your middle family income bracket actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing compared to lower-income peers who face steeper odds. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, lower than the average of 3.21, provided some counterbalance toward passing but wasn't strong enough to offset the top concern. While your low LSAT and early-year decile also factored in, these top three elements dominated the model's reasoning for your specific case. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with higher deciles, GPAs above 3.2, and stronger test scores, your profile aligned more closely with those who historically faced bar challenges. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many from similar backgrounds succeed through targeted effort—but a data-driven call by the model. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average of 36.7, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile standing, and exploring schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories like yours more heavily. Building a standout narrative around your unique journey as a Black male in law could also open doors at institutions prioritizing diversity. With focused improvements, you can reshape how models like this see your future bar prospects.
